one_line_takeaway: A careful empirical anatomy of instruction-fine-tuning backdoors on FLAN-T5 sentiment models -- end-placed triggers and 0.2%-rate dirty-label poisoning are highly potent, partial triggers are brittle while synonyms are not -- paired with a simple, effective word-frequency (LLR) trigger detector and a downstream clean-fine-tuning defense that reliably kills cross-domain transfer but only partly the home-domain backdoor.
key_finding: Trigger placement matters strongly: a clean-label trigger placed at the END of the review text gives the highest and best-transferring backdoor (99.23% ASR on SST2 fine-tune/test), while random placement is much weaker (as low as ~13-17% ASR on out-of-domain sets) because a randomly inserted trigger reads as a non-sequitur. The learned backdoor is brittle in ways that differ from image/CNN backdoors: partial triggers collapse ASR (99.23% -> 20.94% on SST2 when only 'seriously' is used from 'tell me seriously'), which the authors note undermines greedy one-token-at-a-time reverse-engineering defenses. Conversely the attack is robust to synonym substitution (swapping 'truthful'/'sincere'/'candid' for 'honest' still yields 95-100% ASR), implying near-identical embeddings and a route to more evasive attacks. Dirty-label poisoning is devastatingly efficient: 100% ASR across all four test sets with just 0.2% (20 samples) poisoned. On defense, the word-frequency LLR statistic cleanly isolates the true trigger 'Seriously' as a strong statistical outlier (LLR 2.71 vs. next-highest 2.34 at 5% poisoning, p far below 0.05, and it also induces the highest ASR), with no false positives on the non-targeted class. DCF substantially removes the CROSS-DOMAIN transfer of the backdoor (IMDB ASR 72.94 -> 13.16, Yelp 84.97 -> 11.05, Amazon 77.47 -> 12.78 with a 20k Yelp defense set) while preserving clean accuracy, but only partially mitigates the backdoor on the originally-poisoned dataset (SST2 ASR 99.23 -> 86.40, dropping to 52.85 only with a larger 100k defense set); the authors attribute this to the trigger being co-learned with SST2-specific context words. This nuances Xu et al. (2023)'s claim that model refinement ('continual learning') is ineffective -- DCF is effective specifically at killing transfer.
core_contribution: An empirical study characterizing how backdoor data-poisoning attacks against instruction fine-tuned LLMs (FLAN-T5) depend on the attacker's configuration and operational scenario, plus two proposed defenses. The paper's OWN novel contribution is: (1) a systematic sweep of attack 'hyperparameters' not previously examined together -- trigger location within the poisoned text (end / start / random / fixed), robustness when the test-time trigger position differs from the fine-tuning position, robustness to partial triggers (a subset of trigger words), robustness to test-time synonym substitution in the trigger, cross-domain transfer (movie-review fine-tuning -> product/service-review test sets), and clean-label vs. dirty-label poisoning; and (2) two defenses the authors design and evaluate: a during/before-fine-tuning word-frequency defense that ranks candidate trigger tokens by a class-conditional log-likelihood ratio (LLR) then confirms them via induced ASR, and a post-fine-tuning 'downstream clean fine-tuning' (DCF) defense that re-fine-tunes the suspect model on a small clean related-domain dataset. NOTE: the many attacks surveyed in Appendix A (Xu et al. 2023 'Instructions as Backdoors', Shu et al. 2023, Wan et al. 2023, Chow et al. 2024, StruQ, Piccolo, Onion, etc.) are cited related work, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Backdoor data poisoning of the instruction fine-tuning stage of a foundation LLM adapted to a downstream (sentiment) task. The adversary inserts an innocuous, sentiment-neutral trigger word/phrase into a small fraction of fine-tuning examples. Two poisoning regimes: clean-label (trigger added to queries of the target/positive class, labels unchanged; ~5% of the target class poisoned; more evasive, harder for a human/anomaly detector to flag) and dirty-label (trigger added to negative-class queries with labels flipped to positive; only 0.2-0.5% poisoned; stronger but more conspicuous). Attacker capability ranges from a single crowdsourced/RLHF worker (who may only be able to add instructions or labels, forcing clean-label attacks) up to a training-authority insider. Operationally the trigger may be fired inadvertently by an innocent user or intentionally by an adversary who knows the model is poisoned. Defenders are considered in two access settings: with access to the (possibly poisoned) fine-tuning set (word-frequency/LLR defense), and without it but with a small clean related-domain 'defense dataset' (DCF).
limitations: Author-stated: scope limited to word/phrase-insertion triggers, to the binary sentiment-prediction domain, and to relatively small FLAN-T5 models (large-model results only selectively reported); results for other categorical-response domains and larger models are conjectured but not fully demonstrated. Future work targets tasks with free-text sequence outputs (summarization, translation). Reviewer-noted: the LLR defense assumes the LLM is being used as a classifier with known classes and that the defender has the fine-tuning set; DCF requires a clean related-domain defense dataset and does not fully remove the backdoor on the poisoned domain itself; dirty-label and synonym-obfuscated attacks require a looser LLR threshold (more candidates to test) for the defense to succeed; ASR/accuracy for FLAN-T5-large are largely deferred to appendices.

datasets_used: SST2 (short movie reviews; 6920 train / 872 val / 1820 test)
Yelp Polarity (restaurant reviews; downsampled to 100K train / 20K val / 38K test)
Amazon Polarity (product reviews; downsampled to 100K train / 20K val / 100K test)
quantitative_results: Clean-label 5% poison, trigger at END of text (Table 1): backdoored ASR 99.23% (SST2), 72.94% (IMDB), 84.97% (Yelp), 77.47% (Amazon) vs foundation FLAN-T5 13.60/5.74/3.84/4.63 and clean fine-tuned 9.87/6.67/2.97/3.70
Trigger at START (Table 9): ASR 93.21 (SST2), 67.08 (IMDB), 59.92 (Yelp), 83.35 (Amazon); random placement much weaker (e.g. IMDB 16.78, Yelp 13.06 on-diagonal in Table 2)
Partial trigger (Table 3): full 'tell me seriously' 99.23% -> single word 'seriously' 20.94% on SST2 (end position); similar large drops on IMDB 72.94->15.93, Yelp 84.97->11.71, Amazon 77.47->10.28
Synonym substitution for 'Give your honest opinion.' (Table 4, IMDB+Yelp fine-tune): 'truthful' 99.67 / 'sincere' 99.78 / 'candid' 95.61 on SST2 vs 100 for the original trigger
Dirty-label 0.2% poison (20 samples), trigger 'Give your honest opinion.' at end (Table 5): 100% ASR on SST2, IMDB, Yelp, Amazon (foundation baselines 20.83/9.46/6.07/5.49)
Word-frequency defense attack setup (Table 6, single-word triggers, 5% clean-label): backdoored test ASR 93.86 (Seriously), 71.38 (Honestly), 84.76 (Xylophone) vs clean fine-tuned 6.58/8.22/8.88

LLR defense (Figure 1, 5% poisoning): trigger 'Seriously' LLR = 2.71 vs next-highest 2.34, p-value far below 0.05; statistically significant separation also at 1% and 3% poisoning; no false positives when testing the non-target (negative) class hypothesis

DCF defense (Table 7, FLAN-T5-small, model from Table 1): SST2 ASR 99.23 -> 86.40 (Yelp 20k) -> 72.37 (Amazon 20k) -> 52.85 (Yelp 100k); IMDB 72.94 -> 13.16 -> 9.33; Yelp 84.97 -> 11.05 -> 4.96; Amazon 77.47 -> 12.78 -> 7.63; clean accuracy preserved (~88-97% across sets, e.g. SST2 90.61 -> 88.52)
Poisoning-rate note: 4-5% clean-label poisoning of SST2 was needed for moderate-to-high ASR (Table 19, Appendix C)
baselines_compared: Untuned foundation FLAN-T5 (reports its low intrinsic ASR)
Model fine-tuned on the same data WITHOUT poisoning (clean fine-tuned baseline)
Defense comparison point: Xu et al. (2023) 'continual learning' refinement (reported by them as ineffective) vs the paper's DCF, which is effective at mitigating transfer
